coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots coated LiCoO2 for improved cycling stability and thermal safety at high voltage
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yiping
Dong, Houcai
Wu, Kuan
Chen, Xi
Wang, Shaofei
Gu, Wei
Hong, Ziwei
Liu, Min
Shen, Yanbin
Lu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Surface coating is an efficient strategy to enhance the interfacial stability of the electrode. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have advantages of excellent dispersion in water, outstanding electron and ion conductivities and high specific surface area. In this work, GQDs were coated directly on the surface of LiCoO2 particles (GQDs-LiCoO2) through liquid phase method. Compared to bare LiCoO2, the cycling performance, rate capacity and thermo-stability of the GQDs-LiCoO2 have been significantly improved in the voltage range of 3.04.5 V. GQDs-LiCoO2 showed a much higher capacity retention than that of bare LiCoO2 (82.8 % vs 46.1 %) after 100 cycles at 0.5 C. The excellent improvement of the GQDs-LiCoO2 was mainly attributed to the formation of a uniform, stable, dense and well-conductive protective layer on the surface of LiCoO2 particles by the surface coating of GQDs. The detailed analysis of the cycled electrodes reveals that the GQDs coating stabilizes the crystal structure of LiCoO2 and suppresses undesirable interfacial side reactions between the cathode and electrolyte, leading to the improvement of battery performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LiCoO2
GQDs coating
high-voltage performance
thermal safety

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the fabrication of GQDs-LiCoO2 composites.
SEM images of (a) Bare LiCoO2, (b) 0.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2, (c) 0.8 % GQDs-LiCoO2, (d) 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 and (e) 1.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2 (f) TEM image of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2.
EDS mappings of C, O and Co on the surface of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 sample.

At room temperature, in the voltage range of 3.0-4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+) (a) Electrochemical cycling stability of bare LiCoO2 or GQDs-LiCoO2 and (b) Discharge-charge profiles of bare LiCoO2 or 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 at different cycles at 0.5 C. (c) At 45 °C, in the voltage range of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+), long-term cycling performance of bare LiCoO2 or 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 at 0.5C. (d) At room temperature, in the voltage range of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+), long-term cycling performance of bare LiCoO2 or 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 at 2C and (e) 5C and (f) 10C. (g) Rate performance of bare LiCoO2 or 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 samples. (h) CV curves of the electrode materials of bare LiCoO2 and (i) GQDs-LiCoO2 at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s-1 from 3.0 to 4.5 V.
XRD patterns of (a) LiCoO2 (b) 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 before and after 100 cycles at 0.5 C, in the voltage range of 3.0-4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+) at room temperature.
At room temperature, in the voltage range of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+), SEM images of (a) LiCoO2 (b) 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 before and after 100 cycles at 0.5C.
Nyquist plots of LiCoO2 (a) and 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 (b) after the first and 50th at discharged state. (c) The equivalent circuit diagram.
The DSC curves of the LiCoO2 and 1 % GQDs- LiCoO2 with the heating rate of 5 °C/min. Samples charged to 4.5 V at 0.1 C.
The performance comparison between some reported LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2 in this work.
The fitted experimental data from EIS spectrum using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 7c.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots coated LiCoO2 for improved cycling stability and thermal safety at high voltage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface coating is an efficient strategy to enhance the interfacial stability of the electrode. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have advantages of excellent dispersion in water, outstanding electron and ion conductivities and high specific surface area. In this work, GQDs were coated directly on the surface of LiCoO2 particles (GQDs-LiCoO2) through liquid phase method. Compared to bare LiCoO2, the cycling performance, rate capacity and thermo-stability of the GQDs-LiCoO2 have been significantly improved in the voltage range of 3.04.5 V. GQDs-LiCoO2 showed a much higher capacity retention than that of bare LiCoO2 (82.8 % vs 46.1 %) after 100 cycles at 0.5 C. The excellent improvement of the GQDs-LiCoO2 was mainly attributed to the formation of a uniform, stable, dense and well-conductive protective layer on the surface of LiCoO2 particles by the surface coating of GQDs. The detailed analysis of the cycled electrodes reveals that the GQDs coating stabilizes the crystal structure of LiCoO2 and suppresses undesirable interfacial side reactions between the cathode and electrolyte, leading to the improvement of battery performance.

GQDs were successfully coated on the surface of LiCoO2 particles through liquid phase method, and formed a uniform, stable, dense and well-conductive protective layer, which leads to excellent cycling performance and thermal stability.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: LiCoO2 has been a prevailing cathode material in Li-ion batteries for several decades and achieved a great success in the consumer electronics market due to its high voltage platform, great compaction density and high volume energy density [1–3]. In fact, the value for practical reversible specific capacity of LiCoO2 is only half of its theoretical limit (274 mAh g-1) at a cutoff voltage of 4.2 V (Li1−xCoO2, x≈0.5, ~ 4.2 V vs. Li/Li+) [4,5]. In order to further increase the energy density of LiCoO2, a direct way is to increase the cut-off potential of the cell [6,7]. Unfortunately, there are some serious issues limiting the application of LiCoO2 under high voltage conditions. Firstly, the long-term cycling at high voltage usually causes irreversible transformation of the LiCoO2 structure and even destroys the layered structure [6,7]. Another reason is that Co shows a strong oxidizing property at high voltage state and Co4+ ions on the surface of LiCoO2 may cause a series of side reactions with organic electrolyte, which results in poor cycling performance and severe capacity fading [8].

In order to circumvent the above issues, surface modification is one of the most effective strategies. Many materials have been used as coating materials to modify the surface of electrode, including metal oxides [9–11], phosphates [12–14], fluorides [15–17] and Li-containing compounds (LiAlO2 [18], LiNi0.5Mn15O4 [19], etc). Carbon materials could also act as coating materials owing to its excellent properties: wide range of resources, low cost, high electrical conductivity and electrochemical stability [20]. For example, Jaephil Cho et al. [21] reported that the Super P coating can improve both the cycle stability and rate capability of LiCoO2, mainly because that the carbon coating can serve as an electron transport medium to improve the electronic conductivity of the cathode [22]; On the other hand, the carbon coating layer is equivalent to a physical barrier, which can inhibit the side reactions between the electrode and electrolyte effectively [23]. Coating of carbon materials can be implemented through different approaches, including chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [24], sol-gel method [25], and high temperature pyrolysis [26]. However, the cost of CVD is very high. The sol-gel method cannot achieve the nano-scale uniform coating. The inert atmosphere in high-temperature pyrolysis method can reduce LiCoO2.

Here in our work, we proposed a simple and effective method by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The GQDs were successfully coated on the surface of LiCoO2 particles via liquid phase approach. GQDs were dispersed in aqueous solution, which can accomplish mass production readily. A series of comparison of properties between bare LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2 was conducted in detail, such as thermal stability, cycling performance and rate capacity, which reveals the effectiveness of GQDs coating on the surface of LiCoO2 particles. The surface coating of GQDs in our work will provide a broader prospect for the commercial application of LiCoO2 batteries. Simultaneously, our work offers an effective and facile liquid phase coating method for optimizing other electrode materials.

Material synthesis of sulfonated GQDs: Sulfonated GQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method [27]. Specifically, to obtain 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene, pyrene (4 g) was added into HNO3 (320 ml) at 80 °C under stirring for 12 h. After the nitration reaction, the yellow powders were filtered with deionized (DI) water to remove the acid. The obtained yellow 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene (0.4 g) and Na2SO3 (3.15 g) were dispersed with DI water (40 ml). The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the colloids containing sulfonated GQDs were filtered with DI water to remove insoluble product.
LiCoO2 powder was purchased from Hefei Kejing Material Technology Co., Ltd. (Model R767), China. All materials were used as received without further purification.

Four samples, 0.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2 (0.5 wt %), 0.8 % GQDs-LiCoO2 (0.8 wt %), 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 (1 wt %) and 1.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2 (1.5 wt %) were prepared by the liquid phase method. To be specific, taking the preparation of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 composite as an example, 0.83 mL (0.12 g/mL, 0.1 g) of aqueous solution of GQDs was firstly diluted with 10 mL of deionized water and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 0.5 h. Then, 9.9 g LiCoO2 powder was slowly added into the aqueous solution of GQDs. After constantly stirring for 0.5 h at 50 °C, the mixed solution was dried. Finally, the composite materials were heated to 300 °C at 5 °C/min for 3 h and naturally cooled to room temperature, before grinded and saved for use.

The crystal structure of the material was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Cu Kα) in the 2θ range of 10°–80°. The morphology of the samples was observed via field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta 400 FEG) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F2F20S-Twin). Elemental analysis was performed on an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex = 633 nm.

The preparation of positive electrode: first, polytetrafluoroethylene (PVDF, 99.9 %, Aladdin) was dissolved in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP, 99.9 %, Aladdin) and stirred for 0.5 h to form a glue solution for use. Second, acetylene black was slowly added to the glue solution and the mixed solution was stirred for 0.5 h. Then the LiCoO2 powder, which has already been dried overnight under vacuum oven at 85 °C, was added to the glue solution and mixed uniformly for 7 h. The mass ratio of the active material, PVDF and acetylene black is 8:1:1. Finally, the slurry was coated on an aluminum foil with a doctor blade, followed by drying at 80 °C for 12 h. The foil was cut into circular pole piece of 15 mm in diameter.

Cell assembly: the 2025-type coin cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box with a condition of water content less than 0.1 mg/L and an oxygen content less than 1 mg/L. The lithium piece (Tianjin Zhongneng Lithium Industry Co., Ltd.) was used as the negative electrode. The separator was a Celgard 2400 (Celgard). A mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC), ethylene methyl carbonate (EMC), and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) (1:1:1, v/v/v) was dissolved in 1 M of LiPF6 as the liquid electrolyte, the electrolyte was purchased from Doudou chem.

The galvanostatic charge/discharge test was performed in the voltage of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+) using automatic galvanostat (NEWARE) battery tester at different current rates at 25 °C and 45 °C. The samples were pre-activated 3 cycles at 0.1 C before battery cycling. The charge cut-off current is 0.02 C. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing of the discharged half-cell was done on an electrochemical workstation (Bio-logic VMP 300, French) with amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range from 200 kHz to 0.01 Hz. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was also carried out on the electrochemical workstation (Bio-logic VMP 300, French) instrument at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s-1 between 3.0 and 4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+).

The half-cell at 4.5 V full charge state was disassembled in an argon-filled glove box, and the LiCoO2 electrode sheet was taken out and rinsed twice with DMC. Then, the active layer on the LiCoO2 electrode sheet was scraped off and dried for 2 h under vacuum, before it was sealed and stored. The composite powder and the electrolyte (mass ratio is 2:1) were sealed together in stainless steel high pressure crucible (Xiamen Meiting Material Co., Ltd.), and heated from room temperature to 300 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The DSC testing was performed under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The schematic of the GQDs-LiCoO2 synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 . LiCoO2 powder was slowly added to the GQD aqueous solution for stirring constantly. GQDs were physically adsorbed onto the surface of LiCoO2 particles during the stirring process, and then heat-treated at 300 °C in a muffle furnace to obtain GQDs-LiCoO2 composite materials.

The morphology of LiCoO2 material before and after coating was characterized by SEM (Fig. 2a–e). The bare LiCoO2 samples show a regular crystal morphology with particle size of 4.5~9 μm, while the GQDs-LiCoO2 samples exhibit some noticeable small particles and a distinct continuous layer on the surface. The coating layer was further studied by TEM. A uniform protective layer with a thickness of about 10 nm was observed on the modified LiCoO2 as shown in Fig. 2f. TEM images display the detailed structure of GQDs (Fig. S1, Supplementary Information). Some flakes can be seen on the surface of the nanosheet (Fig. S1a, Supplementary Information). The twisted lattice structure (indicated by the red circle) has the same lattice spacing as that of graphene oxide (Fig. S1b, Supplementary Information) [28]. Since GQDs were oxidized from carbon cloth [29], properties of some large-scale flakes in GQDs are similar to those of graphene oxide.

The distributions of the elements on the surface of GQDs-LiCoO2 were studied by EDS elemental mappings. As shown in Fig. 3b–d, the dots assigned to C, O and Co elemental mappings are evenly dispersed on the surface of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 sample (Cu conductive paste used in the test). It can be seen from Fig. S4 (Supplementary Information) that the higher the coating amount, the more obvious the signal of C element is. Crystal structure of bare LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2 samples were confirmed to be the same by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. S3 of Supplementary Information), which reveals that GQDs coating only modifies the surface of LiCoO2.

Fig. 4a reflects the comparison of bare LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2 cycling behavior at a current rate of 0.5 C in the potential range of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+). During the 100 cycles, the bare LiCoO2 shows a significant capacity loss, giving a capacity retention of only 46.1 % (80.12 mAh g-1). Obviously, the GQDs coated samples exhibit a much better cycling performance. Especially for 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2, a reversible capacity of 144.7 mAh g-1 can be attained at the 100th cycle, which holds 82.8 % of the original capacity. In addition, the capacity retention of 0.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2, 0.8 % GQDs-LiCoO2 and 1.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2 is 61.1 % (105.5 mAh g-1), 66.9 % (116.4 mAh g-1), and 76.7 % (135.7 mAh g-1), respectively. For 1.5 % GQDs-LiCoO2, the coating layer may be too thick. The lithium ions diffusion was hindered, leading to a severe capacity declining. All in all, Fig. 4a shows the cycling performance of the bare LiCoO2 and 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 after 100 cycles, where a dramatic difference appears between the capacity of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 and that of the bare LiCoO2. It probably indicates that irretrievable side reactions happen during cycling at a high cut-off voltage.

The side reaction between LiCoO2 electrode and organic electrolyte is one of the crucial mechanisms leading to the failure of LiCoO2 at high cut-off voltage owing to the unstable high-oxidation state Co4+. The highly active Co4+ destroys the crystallinity of the positive electrode, dissolves in electrolyte and accelerates electrolyte decomposition [7]. Consequently, it is of great significance to generate a defensive layer with outstanding chemical and electrochemical stabilities to suppress the side reactions under high voltage [7]. As shown in Fig. 4b, two potential plateaus of the corresponding discharge curve can be manifested at 4.41 V and 3.81 V. The short plateau at 4.41 V is owing to the conversion of the H1-3 phase to the O3 phase [30], while the long plateau at 3.81 V is stemming from the conversion of the two O3 phases [31]. After 50 cycles, the short plateau of bare LiCoO2 disappears, which is probably resulted from an irreversible phase conversion between the O3 and the H1-3 phase in LiCoO2. However, the 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 still has a short plateau after 100 cycles, which indicates that the irretrievable phase transition is impeded.

Fig. 4d–f reflects the comparison of bare LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2 cycling behavior at different current rate of 2C, 5C and 10C in the potential range of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+). During the 100 cycles, the bare LiCoO2 shows a significant capacity loss, the capacity retention of 2C、5C and 10C is 13.2 % (135.7/17.9 mAh g-1), 30.1 % (88.8/26.7 mAh g-1), and 1.8 % (55.4/0.99 mAh g-1), respectively. Obviously, the GQDs coated samples exhibit a much better cycling performance that is 51.9 % (155.9/80.9 mAh g-1), 48.6 % (140.5 / 68.3 mAh g-1), and 43.9 % (104.5 / 45.9 mAh g-1). By the way, as presented in Fig. 4c，when it is tested at 45 °C the initial discharge capacity of bare LiCoO2 is 175.4 mAh g-1 and after 100 cycles, it only remains 97.4 mAh g-1 with a capacity retention of only 55.5% at 0.5 C. In contrary, the initial discharge capacity of GQDs-LiCoO2 is 178.8 mAh g-1, and it still remains 128.5 mAh g-1 after 100 cycles showing a higher capacity retention of 71.9%. In summary, which confirms that GQDs coating greatly enhances the cycling stability, capacity retention and thermo-stability of LiCoO2 and also shows much improvement in compared to what’s reported in most of the reported results [32–37]. The modification methods and the performance comparison between some reported LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2 have been summarized in Table 1 .

The rate capability of LiCoO2 and 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 is shown in Fig. 4g. When the current rate is progressively increased from 0.1 C to 0.2, 0.5, 1 and 2 C, the reversible specific capacities of the 1% GQDs-LiCoO2 are 182.7, 180.8, 172.5, 166.3 and 158.9 mAh g-1, respectively. However, the bare LiCoO2 presents reversible specific capacities of 184.1, 176.2, 158.1, 144.2 and 126.6 mAh g-1, respectively. With different charge/discharge rates, the 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 exhibits a larger and more stable specific capacity than that of the bare LiCoO2, which proves the enhancement of the stability for the 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 at high voltage. After high-rate cycling, when the current is returned to 0.1 C, 96.6 % capacity of the 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 is recovered.

Fig. 4h–i show the CV curves of bare LiCoO2 and GQDs-LiCoO2. According to reports, the CV curves could reflect the phase transitions of LiCoO2 from O3-O2 (hexagonal-monoclinic) and O2-O1 (monoclinic-hexagonal) under a high potential [30,31]. For bare LiCoO2 in Fig. 4h, all redox couples at 4.02/3.85 V, 4.01/3.84 V and 4.0/3.82 V can be distinguished and the peaks shift significantly with the increasing cycles. The redox couples can be credited to the phase transition from O3-O2 and O2-O1 and the peaks shift can be accredited to the instability of the layered structure. In contrast, as for GQDs-LiCoO2 in Fig. 4i, the redox peaks display a much smaller shift, which further certify the enhanced electric conductivity and Li+ diffusion ability and the upgraded stability of the layered structure.

To further illustrate the effect of the GQDs coating on the structural stability of GQDs-LiCoO2 crystals, we compared the XRD results of the electrodes before and after 100 cycles at 0.5 C. As shown in Fig. 5 , we can observe the changes of crystal structure for LiCoO2 (Fig. 5a) and GQDs-LiCoO2 (Fig. 5b). The (003) and (015) peaks of LiCoO2 obviously shift towards a higher angle, which is caused by the irreversible phase transition of the crystal structure and collapse [38]. At the same time, the (003) and (015) peaks of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 show barely displacement, which signifies that the irreversible phase transformation is repressed and the crystal structure is stable.

SEM was used to analyze the morphology evolution of bare LiCoO2 and 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 electrodes. Fig. 6 shows SEM images of bare LiCoO2 and 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 electrodes before and during the 100th cycle. After 100 cycles, the protective layer of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 particles remains intact and well distributed, while the LiCoO2 particles without GQDs coating show visible micro-cracks and an exposed layer structure, indicating that the surface structure of LiCoO2 particles is degraded. Bare LiCoO2 particles are directly exposed to the electrolyte and lack of good solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) protection, which greatly reduces the utilization of the positive active material after a long period of cycling at high voltage.

For a better understanding of the role of the GQDs coating, EIS testing was performed on the half-cells in the discharged state. We can see that the Nyquist diagrams of both the bare LiCoO2 and 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 electrodes possess two semicircles, with one in the high frequency region and the other in the low frequency region (Fig. 7 ). Given the equivalent circuit as shown in Fig. 7c, the high frequency semicircle comes from a combination of the resistances of the surface film (R sf) covering the cathode particles and the corresponding CPE sf. The low frequency one reveals the coupling of the charge-transfer resistance (R ct) with CPE dl [39,40]. The fitted values of R sf and R ct are shown in Table 2 . It can be seen that both R sf and R ct of the electrodes increase with cycling. The growth of R ct is much faster than R sf. As cycle number increases, the R sf values of the samples have a tendency to be stable. There is an increase of the R ct value of the bare LiCoO2 from 417.8 Ω after 1 cycle to 1281.4 Ω after 100 cycles; while that of 1% GQDs-LiCoO2 is from 251.7 Ω to 523.8 Ω. The stability of the R sf values indicates that the SEI membrane generated in the first charge/discharge process is pretty stiff. And the increasing of R ct during cycling implies that interface degradation becomes more serious between the active material and electrolyte, which is caused by the occurrence of side reactions [41]. The EIS results prove that the GQDs coating is useful to suppress the side reactions.

A comparison of DSC traces of the uncoated LiCoO2 and the GQDs-LiCoO2 samples charged to 4.5 V in the electrolyte is shown in Fig. 8 . As can be seen, the bare LiCoO2 sample exhibits an exothermic peak at 253.2 °C, whereas the exothermic peak of 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2 locates at a higher temperature of 263.4 °C. In addition, the intensity of the exothermic peak has also weakened after GQDs coating. The DSC results verify that GQDs coating conduce to a noteworthy improvement in the thermal stability of LiCoO2 materials.

The main purpose of this work is to improve the practical prospect of LiCoO2. GQDs were successfully coated on the surface of LiCoO2 particles by liquid phase method. After the half-cell 100 cycles in the potential range of 3.0–4.5 V (vs. Li/Li+), the 1% GQDs-LiCoO2 can still reach a capacity retention rate of 82.8 %, which is much higher than that of bare LiCoO2 (46.1 %). The specific capacity at 2 C increases from 126.6 mAh g-1 for bare LiCoO2 to 158.9 mAh g-1 for 1 % GQDs-LiCoO2. The analysis done by XRD, EIS and DSC shows that GQDs coating not only enhances the stability of LiCoO2 structure, but also effectively suppresses the side reactions between the positive electrode and electrolyte, thereby greatly upgrading the cycling performance, rate capacity and thermal stability of LiCoO2 materials at high voltage.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Yiping Sun:Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft.Houcai Dong:Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Software.Kuan Wu:Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization.Xi Chen:Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.Shaofei Wang:Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.Wei Gu:Writing - review & editing.Ziwei Hong:Writing - review & editing.Min Liu:Writing - review & editing.Yanbin Shen:Writing - review & editing.Wei Lu:Conceptualization, Methodology, Software.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 21773290, 21625304, 21473242, 21733012), the “Strategic Priority Research Program” of the CAS, Grant No. XDA09010600 and XDA09010303.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Issues and challenges facing rechargeable lithium batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. M. Tarascon, M. Armand, Issues and challenges facing rechargeable lithium batteries, Nature 414 (6861) (2001) 359-367.
C. Liu, F. Li, L. P. Ma, H. M. Cheng, Advanced Materials for Energy Storage, Adv. Mater. 22 (8) (2010) E28-E62.
J. B. Goodenough, Y. Kim, Challenges for Rechargeable Li Batteries, Chem. Mater. 22 (3) (2010) 587-603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-voltage cell performance and thermal stability of nanoarchitectured polyimide gel polymer electrolyte-coated LiCoO2 cathode materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. H. Park, J. H. Cho, J. S. Kim, E. G. Shim, S. Y. Lee, High-voltage cell performance and thermal stability of nanoarchitectured polyimide gel polymer electrolyte-coated LiCoO2 cathode materials, Electrochim. Acta 86 (2012) 346-351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of cathode materials for lithium batteries guided by first-principles calculations
